However, most importantly, without driver controls, it likely won't be able to do some simple things like correctly park in a garage, double park in a driveway, or park in the assigned parking spot.

5. Tesla seems to be ignoring the need for government regulations.

Government regulations could pose an insurmountable challenge. Tesla cannot release its cybercab vehicles to operate in any US city without approval from that jurisdiction. Regulations for autonomous ridehailing services are broad, complex, and varied. Its plan for a nationwide rollout would need approval from all states, which is a tough, if not impossible, task. There are local regulations. Municipalities have the authority to control traffic, limit use, and issue permits for pick-up and drop-off zones. Most even directly regulate autonomous vehicles. Finally, locations such as airports require specific approvals. For example, Waymo is still working on getting approval for trips at the Austin airport. Tesla appears to be simply ignoring the need to get these approvals.

So far, I believe Tesla has grossly failed to live up to its robotaxi predictions. According to Electrek, as of March 16, 2026:

Based on the latest crowdsourced data from independent trackers who reverse-engineered Tesla’s ride-hailing app, the company is operating approximately 35 vehicles in Austin, nine months after launch. Availability remains low — our status check last month found it below 20%.

Every single one of those vehicles operates with an in-car safety supervisor who has a finger on a kill switch. That supervisor is presumably preventing additional crashes beyond what we’re seeing in the NHTSA data — making the actual per-mile crash rate of the unsupervised autonomous system even worse than the reported numbers suggest.

Tesla is also operating a single unsupervised robotaxi that is limited to a small section of the service area in Austin. Musk announced in January that Tesla had started offering rides without a safety monitor, but the “unsupervised” fleet is limited to a single vehicle operating at a time in a very limited part of the Austin service area.

I believe that Tesla will be forced to follow the ARS deployment model, proven successful by other companies, especially Waymo. It will need to deploy the service by metropolitan area, obtain approvals from state and local authorities, geofence routes and destinations until expansion is proven, and provide the service with a Tesla-owned robotaxi fleet. This means that its robotaxi service will be well behind Waymo's, take many years to meet the expectations Musk set, and be extremely expensive, requiring billions in capital investment.

From an investment standpoint, I continue to believe that Tesla is way overvalued at $1.3 trillion. (See: Without Valuing Future Products, What Is Tesla Really Worth?) As discussed in that article, most of its valuation, as much as one trillion dollars, is based on projections for its robotaxi business and humanoid robots. So, in the best (I believe unrealistic) case, a huge robotaxi success is already priced in. In the worst case, there is a 70% to 80% downside risk, as computed in the previous article.

Overall Investment Conclusions

As we start our journey through the AV Investment Roadmap, we see that the most significant transformation in transportation is underway, with autonomous ride-hailing services replacing ridesharing and creating an even larger total available market. However, investment opportunities here are somewhat restricted.

  • Waymo (Alphabet) is clearly the leader, and I project it will be very successful. It may even be worth a trillion dollars over the next five years or so, but even that would be only about a third of Alphabet's current valuation. Nevertheless, it's another reason that Alphabet is a Buy.
  • Zoox is in a similar situation to Amazon as a subsidiary. As the company is in second place in my rankings, if it grew to be worth $500 billion, it would be only 20% of Amazon's value today. However, again, this is a strong supporting reason to buy Amazon.
  • I think Uber's autonomous ride-hailing strategy is the best for it. However, even if it's successful, it will cannibalize its current ridesharing business, so it's unlikely to deliver significant returns to investors. I classify it as a Hold.
  • Motional may have some limited success, but it's a difficult investment.
  • Rivian and Lucid are worth watching to see if these partnerships are positive for them.
  • As for Tesla, it's way overvalued with unrealistic robotaxi expectations. There is no upside, but a substantial downside. For me, it's clearly a Sell and possibly a stock to short over the longer term.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Quantifying The Impact Of The New CMS Payment Increase
data.description:
I have a strong stomach that helps me weather stock market volatility. Maybe the extensive investment time horizon I enjoy plays a big role here too. But I would be lying if I said I have not doubted myself a couple of times over the past year with my UnitedHealth Group (UNH) investment. Almost a year ago, I thought the market was wrong about UNH. Since then, the stock has lost almost a third of its value. A sucker punch by Mr. Market for doubting his valuation of the company.

Last November, I doubled down on my bullish stance on UNH and thought investors should jump on board before buybacks resume.

Some of the projections that I highlighted in my original thesis have not come true. For instance, I expected increased utilization levels to normalize as the demand for elective procedures wanes (many patients delayed these procedures during Covid, leading to pent-up demand in later years). This has not happened. Even in Q4 2025, utilization levels remained elevated. This was a major reason behind UNH's lackluster performance last year.

Then came the loss of members. UnitedHealth had no option but to reprice its books. And this has led to a noticeable loss of members. The company now expects to lose at least 1.4 million MA members this year, bringing the total decrease in policies to at least 2.3 million. This is not good news. As a result of these projections, the company has guided for a 2% YoY decrease in revenue this year.

Amid all these negative developments, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services released its 2027 MA rate announcements yesterday (April 6). For 2027, the average rate increase would be 2.48%, which translates to $13 billion in additional MA payments (incorporating risk trends, the average increase comes to almost 5%). UNH stock gained more than 6% in after-hours trading on this news. This was a major surprise for investors who were expecting a meager 0.09% rate increase based on the advanced notice released by the CMS in January.

In this analysis, I will try to quantify how this rate increase will impact UnitedHealth's financial performance.

Understanding The Impact Of CMS Payment Rate Increase

For 2027, we are now looking at an average rate increase of 2.48%. If you want to fully understand the rationale behind this payment increase, I highly recommend reading the full announcement by CMS, which you can find here.

From a purely financial performance perspective, this payment increase will not have any impact on UnitedHealth this year. However, given that the company is expected to submit its MA bids for 2027 by the end of June this year, we are likely to see management's projections for 2027 margins and growth in a few months. In upcoming earnings calls, UNH management is likely to reveal their projections for these key performance metrics. Therefore, I believe the CMS payment increase for 2027 will have an impact on UnitedHealth's market valuation well before we see any tangible financial benefits.

Coming back to the financial impact, let me first establish the fact that UNH is facing a profitability crisis. Despite topline growth, the company saw its earnings from operations decline 41% YoY in 2025 due to increased costs.

UnitedHealth 2025 performance

The CMS payment increase will help UNH bridge the gap between surging costs and premiums. Also, the company is seeing a massive loss of members due to its repricing strategy, where UNH has cut back on member benefits. The CMS payment increase should help UNH reverse some of this impact, as the company would be in a position to lure new members with more attractive benefit packages. For 2026, the company has decided to secure margins at the cost of growth. Although this sounds like a prudent strategy, UNH cannot afford to lose market share to competitors indefinitely. I believe the CMS payment increase allows the company to be more aggressive with its pricing and, therefore, retain or even grow its market share in 2027.

To better understand the expected financial impact of the CMS payment increase, we can use a simple calculation to single out the positive surprise resulting from the announcement.

Metric Value
Projected MA members by the end of 2026 7.27 million (midpoint of the management guidance)
Projected Medicare and Retirement revenue in 2026 $165 billion
Average annual revenue per MA member $22,696 ($165 billion/7.27 million)
Rate delta 2.39% (2.48%-0.09%)
Incremental revenue potential in 2027 $3.94 billion ($165 billion x 2.39%)
Estimated flow-through rate to operating income 25%
Estimated incremental operating profit boost $985 million

Source: Author's estimates, management guidance

From a total revenue perspective, an incremental $3.94 billion may not look significant given that UNH is expected to report revenue of close to $440 billion this year. However, the real highlight is how the CMS payment increase will boost operating earnings. The company expects operating earnings of at least $24 billion this year. The CMS payment increase, as shown above, can be expected to boost the operating income by an incremental $985 million, or by over 4%. This is assuming a 25% flow-through to operating income, which is a very conservative estimate.

I used a 25% flow-through rate, as I believe UNH will direct the majority of this revenue windfall to win back lost members. This, in my opinion, will be the best use case for the incremental revenue expected from the payment increase. When UNH submits its bids for 2027 in a few months, the company will have full visibility into the ~$3 billion revenue windfall. This will help the company bid aggressively to recapture some lost market share. UnitedHealth was forced to cut back on supplemental benefits this year to avoid an erosion in profit margins. With the revenue windfall from the CMS payment increase expected to hit next year, UNH can be more aggressive in offering OTC allowances and even including dental, vision, and hearing benefits to payers next year. These bids will be submitted to the CMS by June, as I highlighted earlier. The potential addition of supplemental benefits should help UNH lure new customers.

There is also the possibility that UNH will use some of this revenue windfall to cautiously enter new markets and revive its expansion strategy that was put on hold last year as part of its efforts to secure margins. This is because some of these new markets proved to be unprofitable. From a long-term growth perspective, market expansion is essential to ensure sustainable earnings growth.

On the back of these developments and expectations, I am confident in maintaining my bullish stance on UnitedHealth for a few reasons.

  1. I believe the demand for elective healthcare procedures will eventually normalize.
  2. UNH's decision to eliminate unprofitable businesses will boost margins.
  3. With the debt-to-capital ratio inching closer to management's target, a resumption of buybacks looks to be on the cards for H2 2026.
  4. Earnings growth will accelerate beyond 2027 as the company exits 2026 as a leaner business with a focus on profitable segments.

Risks

I am keeping an eye on a few risks that threaten my investment thesis, starting with the uncertain regulatory environment. UNH has been subject to increased regulatory volatility in the past couple of years, and I don't see this environment improving materially in the coming months. Any adverse developments could trigger another market selloff.

Persistently elevated reimbursement costs will hurt margins. Given that I expect the demand for elective healthcare procedures to gradually decrease in the coming quarters, I am keeping an eye on these trends for data that suggests the opposite will happen.

Takeaway

UnitedHealth has been battered for a couple of years, but the tide is finally turning in its favor with the 2027 CMS payment increase. With earnings growth set to revive in 2027, I am thinking of accumulating more UNH shares in the coming weeks. The next couple of earnings calls will provide valuable insights as to how the company is planning to capture some lost market share by offering attractive benefits packages. This could act as a catalyst to drive the share price higher well before UNH reaps the tangible financial rewards of the CMS payment increase.

data.title: Rambus: A Leveraged Play On The AI Memory Bottleneck
data.description:
Overview

Rambus Inc. (RMBS) is a fabless semiconductor company that is focused on designing memory interface chips, high-speed silicon intellectual property and security solutions for chips and data. Rambus’ core focus is on data-intensive computing systems like AI infrastructure, data centers and high-performance computing. Rambus’ positioning is that it helps transfer data faster, more efficiently and securely inside modern computing systems.

Broadly, Rambus Inc has three business segments – memory interface chips (the product business), silicon IP (design and blueprint business) and licensing and royalties (patent monetization).

The key components that Rambus makes include RCD which coordinates timing, PMIC which regulates power, and SPD Hub/Sensors which monitors and manages memory. According to management, memory modules are expected to become more complex which implies more Rambus content per module. The memory interface chips segment is the fastest growing segment for Rambus and is more cyclical based on semiconductor demand. This segment is lower margin than the IP segment but is scaling fast.

In the IP business segment, Rambus provides three types of IP – memory IP, interface IP and security IP. Since designing these high-speed interfaces is complex, semiconductor companies rely on Rambus to have a faster-to-market approach given Rambus has decades of experience which provides it with a massive edge in the IP market. Therefore, one of the greatest benefits of this segment is the scalability.

The last segment that acts as a stable source of revenue is the licensing and royalties which other companies pay to Rambus for using their technology. The figure below provides the revenue breakdown based on each business segment for the third quarter of FY25 and FY24.

The ecosystem integration right from owning the ideas, monetizing them to building on them provides a strong moat for Rambus, in addition to the fact that it already has 2000+ patents.

In today’s time as AI is driving massive data workloads and system complexity is rising, the need for memory system management efficiently becomes ever more important. Moreover, data centers are now facing energy limits and therefore, the need for optimized energy subsystems become ever more important and therefore, Rambus is likely to have a significant structural opportunity.

Rambus is a leveraged play on rising memory subsystem complexity in AI-driven computing, with upside driven by product revenue growth and downside supported by high-margin IP licensing.

With Rambus set to report earnings later this month, key areas of focus include continued momentum in product revenues, DDR5 adoption trends, and management commentary on AI-driven demand.

Tailwind

Memory Super-cycle and Rambus’ Positioning

One of the major developments in the current AI market is the memory supercycle. This is being driven by demand creation as global data creation is projected to reach 181 zettabytes. In addition to this, AI data centers are absorbing a growing share of the global memory supply. According to analysts estimates, AI data centers could consume ~70% of high-end DRAM in 2026.

Note that in such advanced computing systems, processors are faster than memory systems, thereby making memory systems a bottleneck because as compute continues to outpace memory speeds, memory has emerged as the primary system bottleneck. AI workloads for example, require extremely high memory bandwidth. Since memory speed is the bottleneck, memory subsystem performance determines system throughput. Rambus positions itself as one which tries to solve the memory bottleneck problem.

Naturally in response to these forces, prices are rising at rapid rates. DRAM contract prices jumped 50%+ QoQ entering 2026. DDR5, HBM and server-grade DRAM are experiencing sharpest price increases with double-digit QoQ growth as the supply-demand gap widens.

In the recent developments in advanced computing systems, one of the key developments is the memory systems becoming a bottleneck which leaves open a space for Rambus Inc. Every server memory module requires RCD (clock driver) and PMIC (power management).

Currently, Rambus Inc holds 40% market share in DDR5 RCD space. PMIC (Power Management Integrated Circuit) plays the role of managing, converting, and distributing power among components. Rambus has stated that they are targeting a market share of 20% in this space.

Moreover, these developments indicate that rising DRAM prices and complexity indicate more Rambus content per module and also higher margins. Therefore, with memory becoming the bottleneck, it presents a significant and underpenetrated opportunity for Rambus. As the growth of RCD and PMIC continue, it goes on to directly benefit the product business segment of Rambus. This adds into the revenue via volume growth and possibly greater margins as prices continue to grow as well. Moreover, as newer architectures come out, Rambus is likely to win newer IP contracts, enabling further sources of stable cash flows.

DDR Evolution and Growth of High-Bandwidth Memory

Another development taking place in the memory segment is the transition from DDR4 to DDR5. The figure below shows the growth in the usage of DDR5 that is expected by 2028 in servers and data centers.

This implies higher bandwidth, higher density and more complex architecture. Therefore, this will lead to more interface components and more advanced signal management. Note that previously Rambus had a market share of 25% market share in DDR4 RCD whereas in DDR5 RCD they have a market share of 40%.

The bull case doesn’t stop at DDR evolution alone. The growth of high-bandwidth memory is an additional factor that is likely to benefit the business prospects of Rambus. Rambus participates in the HBM ecosystem via IP. Therefore, while the growth of HBM does not directly contribute in terms of the product business, but contributes positively via the IP business. The figure below illustrates that the global HBM market is expected to witness 25.4% CAGR growth in the coming years.

With this rate of growth, it is natural to expect Rambus to derive significant benefits from this segment as this is likely to translate to double digit growth in revenue and profits.

Financials & Valuation

The figure below gives the revenue and profit margins for the past 10 years. The revenue value reached the lowest value in FY19 and began rising since then consistently. The profit margins also followed a similar pattern.

The initial fall in revenue was a result of instability in licensing revenue as Rambus was primarily a licensing and royalty driven business. Moreover, this stage also involved gradual transition to the product based business which led to heavy capex. In the subsequent stages, there was gradual recovery as the product business gathered momentum and was ably aided by the semiconductor industry tailwinds as well. So while the development of the product business may have concerns of per-unit margin compression, the scaling of the product business is likely to allay such fears as evidenced by the trend in profit margins over the past 5 years.

In order to gain an understanding of the intrinsic value per share, it was crucial to perform a valuation. However, given the relatively early stage of the business and the volatility in free cash flows, it was difficult to accurately forecast cash flows to perform a DCF valuation. Therefore, I decided to go ahead with a market-based valuation using peer comparison.

However, given the different nature of the business segments, a single peer-based valuation was not appropriate given the company’s hybrid business model. Therefore, I decided to conduct a sum of the parts (SOTP) valuation to get a more accurate idea of the per share intrinsic value. The figure below illustrates each business segment along with the peer group and multiple selected.

There are three business segments that have been selected and the peer group has been curated accordingly based on the nature of the business. However, since the profits breakdown is not given for each of the segments, I have selected the EV/Sales for the purpose of valuation and tried to estimate the implied enterprise value and summed it to get the total enterprise value. After subtracting net debt, I got the target equity value and that gave the per share equity value. The valuation exercise gave us minimal upside, indicating that the market has already accounted for the potential upside of Rambus. However, I believe that Rambus’ potential lies beyond its peer group and that can be accounted for only through a DCF valuation which is currently difficult because of the volatility in cash flows.

Upcoming Earnings

Note that the upcoming earnings report will prove crucial as there will be several key metrics worth watching out for. Product revenue growth needs to be watched out since it constitutes of largest share of revenue, especially the growth coming from DDR5 RCD shipments.

Moreover, an additional metric worth keeping an eye for will be the gross margin trajectory. As the product business scales, there may be fear of margin dilution. Therefore, any signs of margin stabilization expansion will be closely monitored for in comparison with the earnings outcome of the previous year. Moreover, expectations regarding PMIC based shipments also need to be looked out for as that could prove a key benefit of diversification as well as whether Rambus manages to achieve its advancement towards the growth of 20% market share expansion.

Lastly, what the management expects regarding future AI-related and data-center based expansion could provide an understanding of the future expectations of the sales and the plans of future expansions product business as well for investors. Therefore, the upcoming earnings release will serve as an important validation point for Rambus’ transition toward a product-driven growth model.

Headwinds

Revenue Concentration

One of the greatest risks facing Rambus is customer concentration. According to the annual report, top 5 customers accounted for roughly 58% of the revenue in FY22. This represents high revenue concentration for a semiconductor company given the industry is one which is prone to cyclicity. Therefore, any fluctuation in the end market for semiconductors is likely to have a bullwhip effect on the sales of Rambus which operates higher up the value chain. The customers of Rambus are primarily memory manufacturers and semiconductor & chip designers.

While many of the customers of Rambus are well-established names in the industry, this, coupled with the factor of customer concentration, adds to the risk of pricing power held by the customers. Moreover, if a customer decides to change its strategy and develop in-house capabilities, that would have a severely negative impact on the future sales of Rambus.

Added to this is the factor of geographical concentration as well. According to the FY25 annual report’s data, approximately 70% of Rambus’ revenue comes from Asia. The concentration is primarily in Korea, Singapore and Taiwan. While Asia is the new growth center for the semiconductor industry, the geographical concentration also represents geopolitical risk with exposure to US-China tensions, export restrictions and trade barriers. The customer concentration and geographical concentration adds to the systematic risk and impacts the product business segment especially, which accounts for a larger proportion of the total revenue.

Given the nature of the industry, any kind of volatility negatively impacts the profitability. While these risks are material, Rambus’ hybrid model provides partial insulation through stable licensing revenue, though execution in scaling the product segment remains critical. However, solving for customer concentration remains important.

Risks from Transition

Previously Rambus was a licensing company which is now transitioning into a semiconductor product and systems solution company. This by default indicates a shift towards lower-margin, cyclical and more operationally complex business. The product-based business introduces further layers of complexity like demand forecasting in a cyclical business, inventory management and supply-chain dependence. Moreover, the profit margins are also compressed due to the shift towards the product-based business as the IP segment promises higher margins. For example, product business became 52% of total revenue from 46% of the total revenue within a year. This was accompanied by a 1.2 percentage point decline in the gross margin.

Added to the problem is that of technological obsolescence risk. While Rambus is a fabless manufacturer, updating standards, architecture and technology. This leads to a continuous pressure on Rambus to update its IP and product designs.

However, as industry conditions and Rambus’ readiness have illustrated, it is positioned to benefit from the upcoming trends like the development of DDR5 technology. Therefore, for the upcoming years, Rambus is positioned for strong earnings potential and the high demand in memory technology will ensure that the product business has a potential to scale up as well.

Conclusion

Rambus represents a differentiated way to gain exposure to the structural growth in AI-driven computing infrastructure, particularly at the memory subsystem layer where performance constraints are becoming increasingly critical. As data-intensive workloads continue to scale, the importance of efficient, high-speed and power-optimized memory architectures is rising, positioning Rambus favourably within the semiconductor value chain.

The company’s hybrid business model which combines the high-margin and recurring IP business segment with the rapidly scaling memory interface chip business creates a balanced financial profile. The licensing segment provides stability and cash flow visibility, while the product segment offers operating leverage and direct participation in the ongoing memory technology transition, particularly with DDR5 and emerging high-bandwidth memory architectures.

Moreover, the broader industry trends are expected to favourably benefit the product business which already holds promise based on past financial performance.

However, these opportunities have to be viewed with caution as there are certain risks involved as well like customer & geographic concentration of revenue, the risk of transition and so on. Overall, Rambus can be viewed as a leveraged play on rising memory subsystem complexity, with upside driven by product scaling and downside supported by resilient IP licensing cash flows. Therefore, my recommendation for Rambus Inc will be BUY.

data.title: Lamb Weston May Have Higher Margins In 2027 (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I now think Lamb Weston (LW) is likely to report stronger margins in fiscal 2027. The last time I wrote about this company, it looked like it was fairly valued in comparison to fiscal 2026 expectations. It’s narrowed its guidance, but it looks like its situation might not change much this year. But in fiscal 2027, several short-term headwinds will be gone, including palm oil tariffs and French fry factories operating at low capacities. And Starboard Value also has its own plan to improve Lamb Weston’s efficiency. So, while Lamb Weston hasn’t provided 2027 guidance yet, I think it will be more profitable next year.

In fiscal Q3 2026, Lamb Weston continued to trade pricing for volume. Its volume was up 7%, and its pricing was down 7%. Its gross margin was 21.2%, which was down from 27.8% in the year-ago quarter. This isn’t too surprising. Fast-food restaurants are still slashing their value meal prices to maintain their own volume. And consumers are buying private-label French fries instead of brand-name ones. So, I’d expect Lamb Weston’s margins to remain under pressure. But the company’s SG&A costs also fell 80 basis points from 10.8% to 10.0% of revenue. It sounds like Starboard is focused on reducing Lamb Weston’s SG&A, so the company might already be listening to activist investors.

The bull case for Lamb Weston is about bottom-line improvements. This stock has a 3.86% dividend yield, which isn’t as high as some of the other food companies’ dividends. Other large food companies’ stocks are trading at lower valuations. And Lamb Weston may not be able to raise its prices, either. Right now, its mix is negative because consumers are trading down. But Lamb Weston could still achieve earnings growth in fiscal 2027 because some short-term headwinds will be gone.

Lamb Weston’s Tariff Costs Will Drop in Fiscal 2027

Lamb Weston grows lots of potatoes in the US, so it doesn’t have to import potatoes. It is importing the oil it’s using to fry the potatoes, though. And the government recently reduced its tariffs on imported oil, so that’s one temporary headwind that’s gone now. On the Q3 2026 call, CFO Bernadette Madarieta said, “As Mike mentioned, we expect potato input costs to decline in the upcoming crop year. Most of our tariff exposure relates to imported palm oil. Recent trade agreements eliminated that tariff, which is a positive development for our cost structure going forward.” So, Lamb Weston will have lower operating costs in fiscal 2027 because the tariffs will be gone. As a result, its gross margin should be higher.

Lamb Weston Doesn’t Need to Build New Factories Right Now

It sounds like Lamb Weston will also have more cash available in fiscal 2026 than it expected. It updated its guidance and reduced its capital budget from $500 million to $400 million. So, it sounds like the company has enough French fry factories and production lines for the time being. On the call, CEO Mike Smith said, “Yes. I think, just as a reminder, obviously, we were spending a lot on capital when we were doing the greenfield expansions. And obviously, we have enough capacity in our footprint and don't need that spend.”

I wasn’t expecting Lamb Weston to do this, but this seems like a good decision. When I was doing research for my previous article, it sounded like Lamb Weston’s plants weren’t operating at full capacity, so the company had lower margins. But it sounds like the company is now coordinating with restaurants to predict demand better. And if restaurants expect weak results in 2027, then Lamb Weston might not need to build any more French fry factories right now. And that means it will have another $100 million available for other purposes, such as stock buybacks.

Lamb Weston Does Have Premium, On-Trend Products

Lamb Weston doesn’t just make the regular French fries that are stored in grocery stores’ freezers. It also owns the premium health-food brand Alexia. Alexia’s products are available at high-end health-food stores like Sprouts (SFM) as well as traditional grocery stores. So, if consumers buy Alexia’s French fries because they’re a healthier alternative to regular French fries, Lamb Weston is likely to earn higher margins on those sales. The company didn’t bring this up in its report, but it’s a significant strength.

Starboard Wants Lamb Weston to Sell Its Asian Subsidiaries

Lamb Weston also has another way to improve its margins in 2027, but it’s not clear if the company is actually planning to do this. Starboard wants Lamb Weston to sell its Asian businesses because they have low margins. And Starboard might be right about this. Lamb Weston has two reporting segments: North America and International. In Q3 2026, the North America segment had a 28% adjusted EBITDA margin. For the same quarter, the international segment’s margin was 3.5%. Lamb Weston’s overall EBITDA margin was 17.4% in Q3 2026.

The international segment provides about one-third of Lamb Weston’s revenue but generates very little profit. So, Lamb Weston could sell it, and its EBITDA margin would rise by double digits. Additionally, the sale would help the company pay off its debt. Lamb Weston has $3.64 billion in long-term debt. Its fiscal 2026 guidance calls for $1.11 billion in adjusted EBITDA at the midpoint. That’s a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.28. If it sold its international operations, its EBITDA would be about the same, but it could greatly reduce its debt.

But it would be speculative to expect this, even if it’s what Starboard wants. It sounds like Lamb Weston might not be planning to spend as much on new factories for the time being, but it doesn’t appear to have any immediate plans to sell its APAC businesses or its international segment. Lamb Weston still sees long-term growth potential in many regions, including Europe and Argentina, and may accept lower margins in the short term to become a larger international company later. So, I wouldn’t value Lamb Weston based on events that might not occur.

Negative Mix Is Still a Risk in 2027

It sounds like Lamb Weston isn’t having any problems finding new customers right now. Lots of people still want French fries. But consumers are going to cheap fast-food restaurants and buying private-label French fries from grocery stores. So, Lamb Weston is making more French fries but earning less money when it sells them. And because restaurant prices could rise because of the war, consumers might continue to trade down in 2027. So, Lamb Weston’s margins might remain under pressure because of negative mix.

Updated Lamb Weston Valuation

Lamb Weston narrowed its fiscal 2026 guidance. Now it expects $6.45 billion to $6.55 billion in full-year revenue and $1.08 billion to $1.14 billion in adjusted EBITDA. Its updated guidance still calls for a 17.1% EBITDA margin at the midpoint, at least in 2026. But that includes temporary headwinds such as palm oil tariffs and the write-off of excess potatoes in Europe. And Lamb Weston’s factories also weren’t operating at full capacity in the first half of fiscal 2026 either. It’s too early for Lamb Weston to provide guidance for fiscal 2027. But there are several reasons why this company’s margins could strengthen next year, even if consumers are still trading down.

So, I’d estimate that Lamb Weston’s adjusted EBITDA margin could rise 100 basis points to 18.1% in fiscal 2027. And because its year-over-year revenue growth for the first 3 quarters of fiscal 2026 was 1%, I’d also estimate that Lamb Weston could post 1% full-year revenue growth in 2027 as well. So, I’d project that Lamb Weston’s revenue will rise to $6.565 billion and its full-year adjusted EBITDA would be $1.188 billion.

Lamb Weston is trading at a fiscal 2027 P/E of 13. That’s a premium in comparison to other large food companies. It also has an unusual calendar year, but so do its peers, so a comparison works here. General Mills (GIS) has a fiscal 2027 P/E of 11.37. The Campbell’s Company (CPB) has a fiscal 2027 P/E of 9.77. So, while I think Lamb Weston’s margins might rise in fiscal 2027, its P/E ratio might not rise because it’s already trading at a premium to peers.

If I subtract $552 million in depreciation, I get a forecast of $636 million in EBIT for Lamb Weston in fiscal 2027. With a 25% tax rate, its income would be $477 million. With 139.3 million diluted shares, its EPS would be $3.42. And with a P/E of 13, my one-year target price would be $44.46. Lamb Weston’s price was $40.16 on April 6, so my target price is 11% higher than the current price.

Conclusion

I think other SA analysts have come to similar conclusions about Lamb Weston. This stock was fairly valued in comparison to 2026 expectations. That’s why I said it was a hold last time. But now, there are several reasons to expect that Lamb Weston’s margins will be higher in fiscal 2027. And Lamb Weston hasn’t provided 2027 guidance yet, so these factors aren’t priced in yet. So, I’d upgrade Lamb Weston to a buy now.

data.title: The 8 Million Barrel Oil Gap And What It Means For Markets
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Transcript

Mike Larson: There is one market sector on every investor's mind here; it’s energy. The Middle East conflict is driving the price of crude oil, LNG and energy products like jet fuel and diesel much higher. But the magnitude of the moves varies widely by geography and market, and both the shorter-term and longer-term fallout would depend greatly on things we just don’t know. Most important of them, naturally, is the duration and the severity of the ongoing war. To get the best possible answers, I turned to one of the nation’s leading energy experts.

Our first-time guest here on the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, he shared powerful insights into virtually every subsector of the group and every major trend driving supply, demand, and pricing. And that includes things like the AI boom and the ways we’re powering it as a country, something that we will be talking about long after the guns go silent in the Middle East.

He is Ken Medlock, Senior Director at the Center for Energy Studies, at The Baker Institute for Public Policy, which is located at Rice University. Ken, thank you so much for taking some time out here to chat. I know, I haven’t had you on the podcast before, so, for somebody who is not familiar with your background, why don’t you just take a minute or two to talk about what your history is in the energy industry and what you do there at The Baker Institute?

Ken Medlock: Yeah, sure. So, it’s a pleasure to be here. I currently lead the Center for Energy Studies here at The Baker Institute. We have been ranked the number one think tank on energy and natural resources in the world. Really pride ourselves in being data-centered, grounded in foundational theories, economics, engineering, political science, all of the above, have a very diverse background of fellows and staff working on a variety of issues ranging from oil and gas all the way to sustainability and circularity.

So, we run the gamut. And that's largely because what we try to do is elevate conversations. We do not advocate, but we definitely try to elevate discussions so that rational policy and good decisions can be made.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, I appreciate that background. And in a market like this, an environment like this, certainly trying to keep a level head can be a little difficult. So, with that being said, let's talk about the energy markets here from 30,000 feet up. We all know what's going on in the Middle East and how the conflict is driving the major energy markets. How would you set the stage and describe the state of affairs right now in energy?

Ken Medlock: It's a time of tremendous uncertainty and turmoil, without a doubt. But I have to caution everybody because there's no such thing as normal in energy commodity markets. There's always something that is throwing a pebble in the pond, if you will, and creating ripples. Arguably what's going on right now in the Middle East is throwing a boulder in the pond. So, it's creating a lot of consternation, generating a lot of uncertainty and the impacts are not being felt symmetrically.

So, in the United States, for example, because we are a net energy exporter, because we've got an abundance of all forms of energy really, we're not immune from the price increases that we're seeing globally, particularly in the crude oil petroleum product space, but we don't have to worry about running out, which is a little bit different. On the natural gas front, we're not seeing the same price impacts that you're seeing in Europe and Asia, largely because we're an exporter and we are bound up in terms of how much capacity we have to export. So, even though there is an increased appetite for U.S. LNG because the Qatari gas that normally would flow out into the global market is not at the moment. We can't flow anymore. And so, you actually haven't seen domestic natural gas prices rise. So, there's nuance as well in this time of tremendous uncertainty.

But I'd say the one thing that is probably underappreciated historically in energy but is starting to rise to the top is really how interconnected energy supply chains are. So, there's been a lot of discussion about what's going on in the Middle East right now could trigger a landslide towards electrification and diversification from oil and gas, but there is a word of caution about that. In particular, people don't always internalize how deeply intertwined the supply chains, even for electrification, are with oil and gas.

I say that because about 30% of the world's helium comes from the Middle East. That is currently not moving, which means it's not just about the balloons we blow up at parties for our kids, right? It's also an industrial gas and helium is a gas that really doesn't have a substitute in semiconductor fabrication. So, if you're going to make semiconductors, which are critical for all of the control mechanisms for electric power systems, wind farms, solar farms, this move to electrify, you need helium in order to facilitate semiconductor fabrication and production and movement into that space.

And right now, there's concerns about, and I think there might have actually been one or two issued already, but force majeures related to semiconductor plants in Asia who simply do not have the helium they need to produce what they normally would produce. So, understanding how deeply intertwined these supply chains is vital because helium comes from producing natural gas.

When you think about sulfur and ammonia, right, you're talking about things that are connected to natural gas and petroleum refining, and that feeds into the agricultural sector. And you need those things for nitrogenous-based fertilizers and other sulfur-based inputs in the agricultural sector to increase crop yield, maintain healthy resilient systems. And the disruption that we're feeling has ramifications around the world across multiple things. And so, it's not just an oil and gas story, but it really does signal how deeply intertwined all of these supply chains are.

Mike Larson: Yeah. And how would you compare that to the environment that we had in past crises, Gulf War I, Gulf War II, or even as far back as the Arab Oil Embargo? It's a different world, I guess it's fair to say, correct?

Ken Medlock: It is a very different world. In fact, when we think about the Arab Oil Embargo, you're talking about a global oil market that was less than half the size of what it is today, right? So, not quite as large, significant disruption that triggered a massive shift on the policy front. One of the most lasting and probably substantial shifts on policy was an aim to diversify supply chain.

So, one of the things we can do to mitigate the cost of disruptions is to increase arbitrage, the different ways we can trade, right? And storage, so the creation of strategic stocks is a very robust way to increase arbitrage opportunity. That's why the SPR was born. It's why the International Energy Agency was born and its strategic stocks. It's a way to mitigate or create a buffer for these disruptions, but the global oil markets also expanded radically and there's a lot more sources of supply than there were back then. So, the ability to flex is a little bit greater, but the size of this disruption is unprecedented just in terms of the amount of oil that is being held off the market.

But what that's going to do is trigger a significant interest in diversifying pathways out of the region, but also it's going to signal a lot of interest in diversifying energy systems more generally in ways that are feasible, right? I mean, reality is a binding constraint. So, the fact that these supply chains are all connected, cost matters, affordability matters, it's not just about oil and gas, it's about everything, how it all connects, and diversification will be the name of the game for energy supply chains going forward.

I will point out that the price today that we're living through in the Brent world, if you will, so that's the marker in Europe, right? It's still not quite at the levels we saw in the double spike on the heels of the Russian invasion of Ukraine. And it's also not at the levels we saw for five consecutive years from 2010 through 2015, where the price averaged north of $100 a barrel every single year.

So, while this is a shock definitely, particularly on the amount of barrels that are being held off the market, the price environment in terms of the short-run implications is still not quite what we saw in just the last 15 years. So, to make claims that this is going to have dramatic implications for demand destruction, which is long-term capital decay, if you will, in replacement, I think that's a little bit outrageous at this point.

Mike Larson: Okay. Let's try and talk a little bit about numbers here. I mean, how many barrels a day are we essentially short as a result of what's happening? How much coverage can you get from the SPR here in the U.S. and from some of these other global stockpiles? How long does it last, I guess, to cover that gap or at least some of it?

Ken Medlock: So, I'll start with storage. So, IEA member countries, if they import a required to by agreement hold at a minimum of 90 days of their imported supply. So, what that tells you is that every IEA country, and they're all in compliance if they're importing, has the ability to offset at least ninety days, most a little bit more. And so, there is a buffer there that will allow for the storm to be weathered. The critical thing there is, when do you release, right? Because you don't want to trigger a release every time the price spikes. You want to do it when things really become an emergency because the thing about strategic stocks, if you use them today, you don't have them tomorrow. So, you have to make sure that you use them in the most efficient manner possible.

If you're an exporting country, you're not required to hold strategic stock. So, the fact that the U.S. actually does hold an SPR is interesting because in point of fact, as a member of the IEA, it's not required to, but it does. The max send out from the SPR is rated at about 4.4 or so million barrels a day, so quite substantial. It has never quite reached that level. So, max rating and what is actually ultimately achievable are open questions, right, in terms of whether or not we could actually hit that mark. But the amount that you'll see released from the SPR and from IEA strategic stocks, that's usually done in a coordinated way so that you don't flood the market and you actually use those stocks in as judicious a manner as possible.

Now, in terms of barrels disrupted, on a daily basis on average, and this will fluctuate day-to-day obviously, but on average last year it was around 17, 17.5 million barrels of oil and petroleum products daily that flowed through the Strait of Hormuz. And that has been cut off for the most part. Now, there are some shipments that are moving through in agreement with the Iranian government at the moment, but it's a small amount.

Saudi Arabia has redirected flows across the Arabian Peninsula into the Red Sea via the East-West pipeline, and that was constructed in the wake of the overthrow of the Shah of Iran in the late 1970s and was commissioned in the early 1980s. Rated capacity is 5 million barrels a day, but you can flush the NGL lines that run parallel and add that up another 2 million barrels or so. So, from what I understand, they've actually done that, and they're moving 7 million barrels a day across and loading at the Port of Yanbu in the Red Sea.

So, what that basically means is that you still have disrupted over half of that 17.5 million barrels a day of daily flow. So, you'd need to be able to make up roughly 10 million barrels a day if you were going to offset it precisely from what you're going to use from strategic stocks. But in reality, you're not going to see that amount released because there are demand-side mitigation protocols also taking place.

So, you've seen cuts in refinery runs, particularly in Asia, where a lot of those barrels from the Middle East ultimately land. And so that rationing behavior effectively reduces demand so that you don't need the full amount. And so, on net, you're probably looking at, given the combined rationing, 7 million to 8 million barrels a day that were short. Strategic stocks can make up that delta, but only for a short period of time. And so, the market really has to look to other mechanisms to balance.

And one of the things that you're seeing right now is, we came into this year with a bit of a supply overhang. And so, the market was very robustly supplied. There were some agencies predicting a net surplus of supply of 3 million to 3.5 million barrels a day this year. So, if that indeed was going to be the case, now you're only talking about a roughly 4 million barrel a day gap to fill. Right? So, you could see where the numbers whittle down pretty quickly, but it's still sizable. It's still definitely sizable.

Mike Larson: Yeah. Let me ask you this. I mean, there's a couple of day lag between as we're recording this versus when it'll air. So, as we're talking, Brent's around 108, give or take. What level do you start talking? What price levels do you start talking about, of serious demand destruction possibility or potential?

Ken Medlock: So, that's a great question, and you're right. Tomorrow could bring a completely different environment. There could be a tweet on Truth Social that drops the price by $5 and could go the other way, right? But demand destruction, and I'm an economist, so I speak about it a little differently, right? Demand destruction is not about walking up a demand curve and allowing price elasticity to erode demand because price goes higher.

Demand destruction is about price gets to a level where you're never going to want to use that commodity again and you start to retire capital stocks. So that you truly destroy the basis for demand to ever return to where it was. So, this would be like price rising and the price of the pump – of crude oil rising, then the price at the pump rising, and then consumers in mass going out and trading in their motor vehicles, either for more higher efficiency vehicles or electric vehicles, right?

We saw a plus $100 a barrel range for, like I said earlier, for five years straight. Yet over that window, demand globally grew by 7 million barrels a day. So, we did not see demand destruction in that instance. In this instance, I think there's a little bit different concern because of the actual shortages, physical shortages, that you're seeing in Asia. But I would argue that if you start to see prices come down by the time we get to this summer, by the time we get to the end of this year, a lot of this will be a bad memory because the capital that's in place will not be retired. It will go right back to work.

Now, there are going to be steps taken to provide insurance against future disruptions, right? And that will take multiple forms, including other pathways out of the Middle East region, like pipeline routes, etcetera. But it's also going to take the form of increases in efficiency, ways to think about balancing markets differently by alternative sources of supply. So, you might see an interest in increased production and – exploration and production, I should say, in West Africa, in Latin America where there's some pretty robust opportunities available. So, it's going to be a portfolio approach to dealing with this, like every disruption ultimately lends itself to, but insurance will be the name of the game no matter how long this lasts coming out the back end.

Mike Larson: Got it. You were at the CERA conference recently, so obviously had a lot of conversations with people active in the industry in one form or another. Can you maybe sum up how those conversations went? What are they saying about these developments and how they see the energy market unfolding in the coming month?

Ken Medlock: So, the conversations were interesting, because as a conference planner, I felt for S&P Global because you're putting speaker rosters together, you're developing what the agenda is going to look like. And then, of course, we have Venezuela happened in January, and then this happened on the last day of February, right, with Iran. Talk about shocks to the system. So, they had to flex pretty rapidly to try to bring those discussions into focus. Even where there wasn't tremendous flex because there were programmatic elements that were about other issues, Iran and Venezuela crept in, right?

So, it's the topic du jour, if you will, and rightfully so because it is a major source of uncertainty across the energy landscape. The attitudes varied. In general, I'd say negative, but there were some glimmers of hope that were shared by different conference participants, but I think at the end of the day, there's just so much uncertainty it was hard for people to really digest and speak authoritatively about anything.

And so, you found a lot of leaning back into what research have I done in the past? How does this look like or feel like something that's happened in the past? Even though it is starkly different in many ways just because of the physical barrel, that disruption is larger than we've ever seen. So, uncertainty ruled the discussion, if you will, but you could see the wheel spinning. There's a lot of work going on behind the scenes to figure out how to deal with this disruption.

Mike Larson: Well, obviously, the war and its impact on the markets has to be the biggest point of this conversation, but I'd be remiss to, if I didn't come up or bring up a few other topics in energy that would be of greater interest if, for some reason, we were able to get a peace deal tomorrow. And that would be things like the impact of AI-driven demand and power on U.S. resources, natural gas in particular. Any thoughts on, again, if the war eventually moves to the back pages, how are some of those developments going to impact the energy industry here in the U.S.?

Ken Medlock: That's also a great question. In fact, there's been a lot of discussion about how the war is impacting data center build-out because there's concern about natural gas prices and how that feeds into electricity prices and what will data centers do, particularly those that have already made decisions to site co-located natural gas generation at these massive facilities. I mean, you're talking about one facility up in Ohio, I believe it is, that's planning to build something like 9 gigawatts of power generation. There's one in Louisiana that's on the 5 gigawatt, and it's all gas generation, right? Mind you. So, this is a lot of load.

The biggest impediment for the United States is going to be infrastructure development. Can we build the pipelines to move gas from where it's cheap to where it's not? Right? So, over the last roughly month, I believe, the price at Waha, which is the hub price in West Texas, has not traded in positive territory, which tells you there's just way too much gas there that can't move to find a home. So, it really is about building infrastructure so that you can move that gas from where it's not needed to where it is so that you can reduce the price impacts of expanding infrastructure behind those constraints.

But in the power space, you're seeing data centers really go to more co-located generation and less grid-connected generation for two reasons. It does help overcome concerns about spiking electricity prices in the locality where these are sited, right? Because they're generating their own power. But it also speeds up the time to which they can develop because if you're co-locating generation and you're not grid connected, you don't have to get in this massively long interconnect queue where studies have to be done to assess the impact on the grid of a large load being located in a particular place on the grid, right?

So, data centers are thinking about how to move more quickly because the demand for what they provide is not going down. It's going up. It's increasingly going up. And interestingly, and I haven't seen any data on this, but connecting back to the conflict in Iran, I've seen a lot of AI-generated graphics lately on what's going on, where resources are located, how they're sourced, where they end up landing. So, I would hazard a guess that the demands on AI are actually going up as a result of this event, not going down.

So, there's without a doubt a need for this stuff, but local acceptance is the other big issue. You're seeing in some places around the United States a pushback, right? And that manifests ultimately in a regulatory pushback, not just a pushback by local residents. And in other places, you're not seeing that pushback. And so, I'm going to – I think what that's likely going to lead to is clusters forming, large industrial clusters forming where you'll see data centers co-located with access to energy infrastructure. So, this can be wires, generation, natural gas pipelines, co-located near nuclear power facilities. We've seen some elements of that rising as well.

Access to water, plenty of land, and communities that are accepting of siting there. Because typically when you have these industrial clusters form, it does change basically the tax reality in the region. And you start to see schools getting refurbished and new stadiums being built and all things that the local community likes. So, where you see pushback, you're also going to see acceptance in other places, and likely that's going to trigger clusters forming. So, it's going to be interesting to watch this unfold.

Mike Larson: Got it. The tail end of our conversation here, and since you mentioned it, I was going to ask it anyway, and that would be the outlook for nuclear power. How much of a solution is that for not just the AI-driven demand, but the U.S. power industry in general? Some quick thoughts on that, how you see that evolving?

Ken Medlock: Yeah. Nuclear power is intriguing. There's really no way if you want to put your long run hat and think about 25, 30, 40 years from now. There's no way to really think about a fully decarbonized power system without nuclear power because it provides baseload controllable generation that is necessary for any grid. The one thing for reliability that people sometimes forget and any system operator will tell you this or any marketer in the power generation space will tell you this, it's about controllability. And nuclear provides you a base that you can build on that is very controllable, very reliable. Once it's on, it's on. So, when you think about the long run, it has a massive role to play.

Now, how that manifests is anybody's guess. It's probably not going to be large-scale utility power that is incredibly expensive, often runs into local opposition, doesn't really have the same economic prowess that a lot of other smaller, lighter, less capital-intensive generation sources have. But this is where advanced nuclear technologies come into play, where you get into small modular and micronuclear technologies, and we know how to do it. That's not the issue.
